stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{b}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]]) \) and \( \mathbf{b}_2 = ([[c]], [[d]]) \) form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^2 \). Write \( \mathbf{w} = ([[w1]], [[w2]]) \) in this basis as \( \mathbf{w} = x\,\mathbf{b}_1 + y\,\mathbf{b}_2 \). What is the coordinate \( x \)?
solution: Solving \( x\,\mathbf{b}_1 + y\,\mathbf{b}_2 = \mathbf{w} \) gives the coordinate \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the other coordinate \( y \), not \( x \).
This is the first standard component of \( \mathbf{w} \), not its coordinate in the basis.
This is the second standard component of \( \mathbf{w} \), not its coordinate in the basis.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the other coordinate.
This is off by two from the correct coordinate.

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{b}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]]) \) and \( \mathbf{b}_2 = ([[c]], [[d]]) \) form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^2 \). A vector \( \mathbf{w} \) has coordinates \( ([[k1]], [[k2]]) \) relative to this basis, meaning \( \mathbf{w} = [[k1]]\,\mathbf{b}_1 + [[k2]]\,\mathbf{b}_2 \). What is the first standard coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \)?
solution: Substitute the basis vectors into \( \mathbf{w} = [[k1]]\,\mathbf{b}_1 + [[k2]]\,\mathbf{b}_2 \): the first standard coordinate is \( [[k1]]([[a]]) + [[k2]]([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the SECOND standard coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \), not the first.
This adds the basis coordinates directly instead of forming the linear combination.
This subtracts instead of adds the second term.
This swaps which coordinate multiplies which basis vector.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This adds the first components of the two basis vectors, ignoring the coordinates entirely.

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{b}_1 = (1, [[p]], [[q]]) \), \( \mathbf{b}_2 = (0, [[s]], [[t]]) \), and \( \mathbf{b}_3 = (0, 0, [[u]]) \) form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). The vector \( \mathbf{w} = ([[w1]], [[w2]], [[w3]]) \) is written as \( \mathbf{w} = x\,\mathbf{b}_1 + y\,\mathbf{b}_2 + z\,\mathbf{b}_3 \). What is \( x + y + z \)?
solution: Solving the system \( x\mathbf{b}_1+y\mathbf{b}_2+z\mathbf{b}_3=\mathbf{w} \) gives the coordinates \( x,y,z \), and \( x+y+z = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the coordinate \( z \) from the sum.
This omits the coordinate \( y \) from the sum.
This omits the coordinate \( x \) from the sum.
This sums the STANDARD coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \) instead of its coordinates in the basis.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.

stem: Let \( B = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2\} \) with \( \mathbf{b}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]]) \), \( \mathbf{b}_2 = ([[c]], [[d]]) \), and \( C = \{\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2\} \) with \( \mathbf{e}_1 = ([[p]], [[q]]) \), \( \mathbf{e}_2 = ([[r]], [[s]]) \) be two bases of \( \mathbb{R}^2 \). A vector \( \mathbf{w} \) has coordinates \( ([[k1]], [[k2]]) \) relative to \( B \) (so \( \mathbf{w} = [[k1]]\mathbf{b}_1 + [[k2]]\mathbf{b}_2 \)). What is the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \) relative to the basis \( C \)?
solution: First convert to standard coordinates: \( \mathbf{w} = ([[w1]], [[w2]]) \). Then solve \( x\,\mathbf{e}_1 + y\,\mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{w} \) for the coordinate relative to \( C \), giving \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \mathbf{w} \)'s coordinate in the ORIGINAL basis \( B \), not the new basis \( C \).
This is the other \( B \)-coordinate, not the requested \( C \)-coordinate.
This is the first STANDARD coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \), not its coordinate in \( C \).
This is the second standard coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \), not its coordinate in \( C \).
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.
